FACTS
Councilrnenber DeWitt has recently purchased a mobilehome in a
nobj.Iehome park located in the city of carson. She paid fair
market value for the nobilehone. she is renting the space on
which the mobilehorne is located on a month-to-month basis and has
not signed a written 1ease.
The city of carson currently regu)-ates rents that mobilehome
parks may charge tenants for renting spaces in the parks. Under
the city;s ordinance, the carson Mobilehome RentaI Review Board
(the ,rb6ardr') , which is not conposed of menbers of the city
council, establishes the permissible rents individually for each
park. ihe board utilizes no set fornula or inflationary factor in
establishing the rents. It bases its decisions on a number of
factors including changes in the consumer Price Index. The
decisions of the board are final and cannot be appealed to the
city counc i I .
The city's ordinance current).y contains a vacancy control
provision which does not permit landlords to raise the rent on the
lpace upon a change of tenants. This provision has not been
amended- since the adoption of california civil 
code Section
'798.L7.
As originally adopted, section 79a.L7 perrnitted park owners
to offer owners of mobilehones a long term lease (more than 12
nonths) exemPt from local rent control. 
owners had the right to
accept or reject such a lease pursuant to Civj-I Code Sections
798.L7 and 798.18.
section 7ga.L7 lras anended effective January 1, 1991, to
authorize park owners to require prospective purchasers of
mobilehomel to sign long tern leases exenpt from rent control as a
condition of tenancy. it thus removed the abi'tity of the city to
enforce j.ts vacancy control provision. Just recently, senate BiIl
132 was adopted and signed by Governor l'lilson as an energency
measure effLctive May 10, 1991. This bill repealed the amendrnent
allowing park ovrners to demand that ne$/ nobi.lehone purchasers sign
Iong term leases exenpt from rent control.
Sone park owners clain that because prospective. purchasers
are not "honeowners, " as that ter:n i-s defined in civil

Section 7ga.g,3 th"y can be required to sign an exempt lease as a
condition of tenancy. The city betieves that this claim is
erroneous because civil code section 79a.74 strictly 
limits the
grounds for denying a prospective purchaser of a mobilehome a
fenancy in the park and faiture to sign an exelflpt lease is not a
permitled ground. The city has, with the exception of the period
-between January 1, 1991, and llay 10, 1991, when the law authorized
park orrners to require an exenpt lease, unifor:nJ'y interpreted and
ipplied its ordinance to controL rent upon a vacancy and continues
to do so.
The city council has the authority under Governnent code
Section 65863.7 and the city of Carson Municipal code to condition
mobilehome park closures by requiring park owners to provide
reasonable ielocation benefits to horneowners displaced by the
closure. Pursuant to those procedures, the city council may
impose a variety of conditions upon a change of use, including but
.,oL lirnited to provision for payment of the cost of rnoving
rnobilehones to new sites and other reasonable costs of relocation.
The city knows of no present intention by the owner of the
mobileh6rne park in which councilnernber Degitt's home is ]ocated to
close that park. The owner has stated that he has no intention of
closing it in the foreseeable future-
The population of the city, as determined by the latest
census, :,2 az,zoz. There are -urrently 24,777 dv,elling. units in
the city, of which 2,632 are mobilehome spaces. Mobilehornes,
therefoie, constitute approximately eleven percent of the number
of dwelling units in the city. 
The city has no figures for the
nurnber of persons residinq in mobilehones, but it is likely that
iti" 
figr.L srould be less than ten percent of the entire city
population.
You are the Assistant city Attorney for the city of carson'

her duties and respons ibi I it ies under the Act'

Makinq, Participatinq in Makinq' or Attemptinq to Influence a
covernmental Decision
A public official 
roakes a governmental decision or
participates in the making of a governmental decision whenever the
public official- votes on a matter, comrnits the agency to a course
of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behaLf of
the agency, (Regulation 18700(b).) AdditionaIIy, a pub3-ic

or designated

of which is to influence the decision.

The effect of a decision i,s reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantiat likelihood that it will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be nore than a rnere
possibility; 
however, certainty is not required.

of interest; it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
